rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM WEST JR. et al VS. CONTEMPORARY SERVICES CORPORATION, A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Unopposed Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement And Conditional Certification Of Class For Settlement Purposes; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 15, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF JOHNSON, ANGELA'S Unopposed Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement And Conditional Certification Of Class For Settlement Purposes. In light of plaintiffs' further evidentiary showing, including the declarations of Julie Green of CPT Group, Inc. and plaintiffs' counsel Michael D. Seplow, which fully address the Court's concerns, plaintiffs' motion for preliminary approval of class action settlement and conditional certification of class for settlement purposes is granted. Counsel is requested to appear to set a date for the final approval hearing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Tara Materials, Inc. Notice Of Hearing On Renewed Petition For Order Correcting Or Vacating Arbitration Award (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 15, 2019, Line 2, Petitioner Tara Materials, Inc.'S Renewed Petition For Order Correcting Or Vacating Arbitration Award (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) There was no evident miscalculation in the award. "The miscalculation, to be evident, must appear on the face of the award [citation] or be so readily apparent from the documentation in the case that explanation by proofs is not necessary." (Svertson v. Williams Construction Co. (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 86, 94; see also Lopes v. Millsap (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 1679, 1683-1684, 1687 & fn. 7 [same; arbitrator advised parties that he had made a "computation error" in the initial award].) Here, the purported miscalculation is not clear on the face of the final award. The panel also concluded that Tara improperly based this argument on new evidence that was not previously presented. (See Order No. 5, pp. 2-3.) The panel did not refuse to rule on a submitted issue. The panel explicitly took into account the $639,125 amount to which Tara claimed to be entitled in payment credits in calculating the balancing payment. (See Order No. 5, pp. 3-4.) Further, ATI shows that Tara disclaimed seeking monetary relief from it in the arbitration. Thus, the panel did not fail to decide an issue committed to it for resolution. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the tentative ruling.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Renewed Petition For Order Correcting Or Vacating Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 15, 2019, Line 2, PETITIONER TARA MATERIALS, INC.'S Renewed Petition For Order Correcting Or Vacating Arbitration Award. (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling.) Petitioner Tara Materials, Inc.'s petition to correct or vacate arbitration award is denied. The panel did not exceed its powers in making the balancing payment. The arbitrators were charged with dissolving FAE and dividing its assets and they merely made an offset based on their estimation of the differing values of Tara and ATI. They found the balancing payment to be supported by the weight of the evidence, including the testimony of ATI's witness, Suzanne Heinemann, the only damages and accounting expert who testified at the arbitration. In Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. v. Intel Corp. (1994) 9 Cal.4th 362, the Court noted that "the deference due an arbitrator's decision on the merits of the controversy requires a court to refrain from substituting its judgment for the arbitrator's in determining the contractual scope of those powers. [Citations.] Giving substantial deference to the arbitrators' own assessments of their contractual authority is consistent with the general rule of arbitral finality we recently reaffirmed . . . ." (Id. at 372-373.) Here, the remedy the panel awarded unquestionably "bears a rational relationship to the underlying contract as interpreted, expressly or impliedly, by the arbitrator[s] . . . ." (Id. at 367.) The arbitrators' powers "were not expressly limited by any term in the contract," and their award therefore is not reviewable. (Epic Medical Management, LLC v. Paquette (2015) 244 Cal.App.4th 504, 511-512 [arbitrator did not exceed her powers by finding that parties had modified their agreement regarding calculation of medical management company's fee].) Bonshire v. Thompson (1997) 52 Cal.App.4th 803, on which Tara relies, is distinguishable because the contractual arbitration provision in that case, unlike the one in the parties' MOU here, contained a specific limitation on the arbitrator's powers-a prohibition against the introduction of extrinsic evidence-which the arbitrator violated. (Id. at 806, 808-811 ["In light of that limitation, we cannot say that an award based on a term, which extrinsic evidence established was omitted from the contract, is rationally related to the contract"].) Further, Tara effectively forfeited any challenge to the calculation by failing to present an expert witness or other testimony on damages issues, and by failing to comply with the panel's order in the interim award to prepare a proposed final accounting and financial statements. (Order No. 5, pp. 2-3; Final Award at 16-18.) In any event, the panel's determination that Tara did not inappropriately overcharge FAE for a particular year is not necessarily inconsistent with a finding that the charges were high for that year and needed to be "normalized." (Dakak Dec., Ex. 3.) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LA-KIYSHA JOHNSON VS. MARVIN ZUNIGA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 15, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Motion To Compel And Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: HEARING REQUIRED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 15, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT COSTCO WHOLESALE CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling Only.) Defendant Costco Wholesale Corporation's demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the first, second and fourth causes of action, and sustained with leave to amend as to the third cause of action. The court rejects Costco's res judicata argument. Plaintiff has filed two prior lawsuits in the small claims division of this Court arising out of the same incident. Although such conduct is not to be encouraged, the court follows the more recent authority holding that small claims judgments do not have a preclusive effect on subsequent actions as a matter of law, although it recognizes there is a split of authority on the issue. (See Sanderson v. Niemann (1941) 17 Cal.2d 563, 573-574; Sanders v. Walsh (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 855, 865-871; compare Pitzen v. Superior Court (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 1374.) The demurrer to the first cause of action for violation of the Unruh Act is sustained without leave to amend. An Unruh Act claim may be resolved on demurrer "when the business practice appears to be valid on its face as bearing a reasonable relation to appropriate commercial objectives for a public enterprise." (Lazar v. Hertz Corp. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1509.) Here, plaintiff admits that she spat on another Costco customer before that customer touched her, which constituted the crime of battery. (Amended Complaint, Para. 4; see People v. Hamilton (2009) 45 Cal.4th 863, 934 ["evidence that defendant spat upon [a sheriff's deputy] was sufficient for jurors to find defendant committed a battery"].) Plaintiff's speculative allegation, on information and belief, that the Costco manager, "who is also African American," "sided with the African American customer concerning the incident solely because of his race and sided against the Plaintiff because she was an elderly Asian American" (Amended Complaint, Paras. 6, 7) are inconsistent with that admission, and are insufficient to state a cognizable claim for intentional discrimination under the Unruh Act. "Clearly, an entrepreneur need not tolerate customers who damage property, injure others, or otherwise disrupt his business. A business establishment may, of course, promulgate reasonable deportment regulations that are rationally related to the services performed and the facilities provided." (In re Cox (1970) 3 Cal.3d 205, 217; see also Ross v. Forest Lawn Memorial Park (1984) 153 Cal.App.3d 988, 993 ["Under Cox, a business may ask disruptive persons to leave their premises"].) The demurrer to the second cause of action for elder abuse is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff does not allege intimidating or harassing behavior within the meaning of Welfare & Institutions Code § 15610.53. Rather, plaintiff merely alleges that Costco's manager addressed her in a "dismissive and unsympathetic manner." (Amended Complaint, Paras. 6, 16.) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Costco Wholesale Corporation Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 15, 2019, Line 11, Defendant Costco Wholesale Corporation's Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) The demurrer to the third cause of action for loss of monetary value of Costco membership is sustained with leave to amend to plead a viable cause of action that is recognized by California law. Plaintiff may have a claim for breach of an oral or written contract that would entitle her to recover the unused portion of her membership fee and the monetary value of credits she had on account (although it is doubtful that she could recover "consequential economic damages," such as the additional money she claims to have expended to purchase items at higher prices at other retailers or the costs of transportation to those locations). The demurrer to the fourth cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff fails to allege outrageous conduct and therefore cannot plead a claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress. "Liability has been found only where the conduct has been so outrageous in character, and so extreme in degree, as to go beyond all possible bounds of decency, and to be regarded as atrocious, and utterly intolerable in a civilized community." (Cochran v. Cochran (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 488, 496.) There is no separate cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress, and plaintiff also fails to plead sufficient facts to support the negligence claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the tentative ruling.) =(302/EPS)
